cues.x: Bad Blood, by John Carreyrou.
Imagine a young woman standing on a stage in a black turtleneck, her voice pitched low and steady, telling a rapt audience that she is going to change the world.
She holds up a small device, a box no bigger than a desktop printer, and she says that inside it lies the future of medicine.

One tiny drop of blood from a finger prick, she says, and this machine will run hundreds of tests, catch disease before it becomes deadly, and put the power of the laboratory into the hands of ordinary people.
The room believes her.
Presidents believe her.
Billionaires believe her.
Magazine editors put her face on their covers and call her the next Steve Jobs.
And almost none of it is true.
This is the story John Carreyrou tells in Bad Blood, and it is one of the most staggering tales of fraud in the history of American business.
It is a thriller, except every character is real, and real patients paid the price.

Let me take you through it from the beginning, because to understand how it all fell apart, you have to understand how seductive the dream was, and how many smart, powerful people wanted so badly to believe it.
Our central figure is Elizabeth Holmes.
She grew up in Washington, D.C., and later Houston, in a family that carried the weight of old prestige and faded fortune.
Her ancestors had money once, and Elizabeth absorbed the idea early that she was meant for greatness, that she would build something enormous and leave her mark on history.
As a child she told a relative that what she really wanted was to be a billionaire.
Not a doctor, not a scientist.
A billionaire.
She was intense, focused, and unusually driven, the kind of person who set a goal and bent everything in her life toward it.
She went to Stanford to study chemical engineering, and she fell under the spell of a professor named Channing Robertson, who saw in her a spark he found extraordinary.
She worked in a lab, she spent a summer in Singapore, and she came back with an idea that would consume the rest of her life.
The idea was beautiful in its simplicity.

Blood testing, as it existed, meant needles, vials, a technician drawing tube after tube from your arm, then shipping those vials to a central laboratory where expensive machines processed them over hours or days.
Elizabeth hated needles, and she believed most people did too.
What if, she thought, you could take a single drop of blood from a finger prick, the smallest possible sample, and run every test a person might need?
What if the machine to do it was small enough to sit in a pharmacy, or even in someone's home, giving instant results?
You could catch cancer early.
You could monitor a sick child without traumatizing them.
You could, she said again and again, save lives and let no one say goodbye too soon.
It was a vision so appealing that people stopped asking whether it was actually possible.
Because here is the thing that experts knew and that Elizabeth either did not understand or chose to ignore: a single drop of blood from a finger is not the same as blood drawn from a vein.
When you prick a finger, you get a mixture contaminated with fluid from the tissue and cells, and the tiny volume makes many tests wildly imprecise.
To run hundreds of different tests, you need a certain minimum amount of blood, because each test consumes some of the sample.
The laws of chemistry and biology were not going to bend just because a charismatic young woman wanted them to.
But Elizabeth had decided the vision was right, and she treated the science as a mere engineering problem that hard work and belief would eventually solve.
She dropped out of Stanford at nineteen.
She took the money her parents had saved for her tuition and poured it into a company.
She founded it in two thousand three, and she gave it a name that fused two words: therapy and diagnosis.
Theranos.
From the very beginning, the branding was flawless and the substance was thin.
She had a vision, a name, and an unshakable conviction.
What she did not have was a working machine.
To understand Elizabeth Holmes, you have to understand her obsession with Steve Jobs.
She idolized the Apple founder to a degree that went far past admiration into imitation.
She adopted the black turtleneck as a uniform, wearing it every single day, exactly as Jobs had.
She hired designers and engineers from Apple.
She wanted her device to have the sleek, minimalist beauty of an iPhone.
She called one of her products the Edison and later dreamed of a machine as revolutionary as anything from Cupertino.
And there was the voice.

People who knew Elizabeth noticed that she spoke in a deep baritone, unusually low for a woman her size, and there were moments, when she was caught off guard or had a few drinks, when her voice would slip up into a more natural, higher register.
Many who worked with her became convinced the deep voice was a deliberate affectation, a performance of authority and gravitas, another piece of the character she was building.
She had a way of locking her wide blue eyes onto you and barely blinking, a stare that people found either mesmerizing or unnerving.
She was, in short, playing the part of a visionary founder with total commitment, and she played it well enough to fool nearly everyone.
The trouble was always the machine.
The first device Theranos built was called the Edison.
The concept was that a cartridge would hold your blood sample, and the device would perform the tests and read the results.
But the Edison could only really do one narrow class of tests, and even those it did poorly.
The results were inconsistent, drifting, unreliable.
Engineers would get it working in a demo and then watch it fail an hour later.
The robotic arm inside would move a pipette to the wrong spot.
Samples were too small.
The chemistry did not hold up.
Year after year, the machine did not do what Elizabeth promised the world it could do.
And rather than confront that reality, rather than slow down and level with investors and partners, the company began to lie.
This is the heart of the fraud, and Carreyrou lays it out with devastating clarity.
When Theranos needed to run the huge menu of tests it advertised, and its own devices could not do it, the company turned to a dirty secret.
In the back rooms of its laboratory, Theranos ran patient samples on ordinary, commercially available blood analyzers made by established companies like Siemens.
These were the very machines Elizabeth publicly scorned, the big conventional analyzers she said Theranos was going to make obsolete.
Except that finger-prick samples were far too small to run on those machines, which were built for normal blood volumes.
So Theranos technicians would dilute the tiny finger-prick samples with saline to create enough liquid to run them.
And diluting a blood sample throws off the readings, pushing some measurements outside the range the machine is even designed to detect accurately.

So you had a chain of compromises stacked on top of each other: too little blood, diluted to make it fit, run on a hacked commercial machine that was never meant to process it that way, producing numbers that could be wildly, dangerously wrong.
This was the great trick behind the curtain.
The revolutionary technology was, in large part, other companies' machines being pushed to do something they were never designed to do, alongside proprietary devices that did not work.
While the science floundered, the culture inside Theranos grew toxic and strange.
Elizabeth ran the company on fear and secrecy.
Departments were siloed from one another, forbidden to share information, so that no single group of employees could see the whole picture and understand how little was actually working.
The engineers building the device were kept apart from the chemists, who were kept apart from the people running the lab.
Secrecy was justified in the name of protecting trade secrets from competitors, but its real effect was to prevent anyone inside from connecting the dots.
Employees who asked hard questions, who raised concerns about accuracy or safety, were branded as disloyal, as not believing in the mission.
And then they were gone.
Firings were constant and brutal.
People would come to work and find a colleague had vanished overnight.
The message was unmistakable: fall in line, keep believing, or be cast out.
Elizabeth demanded not just work but total devotion, and she surrounded herself with people who reinforced her version of reality and purged those who challenged it.
Enforcing that culture was a man named Ramesh Balwani, who everyone called Sunny.
Sunny was almost twenty years older than Elizabeth.
They had met when she was a teenager, before she started Stanford, during that summer in Asia, and they had been in a romantic relationship for years.
But that relationship was a secret from the board and from most of the company.

Sunny had made money in the dot-com boom, selling a software company before the crash, and he carried himself as a wealthy, successful businessman, though his technical and scientific knowledge was thin.
He came into Theranos and became its president and chief operating officer, and he functioned as Elizabeth's enforcer.
He was harsh, intimidating, prone to belittling employees, quick to fire, and obsessed with rooting out disloyalty.
He tracked how long people spent in the building.
He confronted employees he suspected of leaking or doubting.
Together, Elizabeth and Sunny created an atmosphere of paranoia and dread.

The fact that the company's two most powerful people were secretly a couple, hiding a fundamental conflict of interest from the board and the investors, was itself a serious deception layered on top of all the others.
There is a genuinely tragic thread running through this story, and it belongs to a scientist named Ian Gibbons.
Ian was a British biochemist, an accomplished, thoughtful, deeply knowledgeable man, one of the few real scientific heavyweights Theranos ever employed.

He had worked with Channing Robertson and joined the company early, and he understood, better than almost anyone, the enormous gap between what Elizabeth was promising and what the technology could actually do.
He tried to do honest science.
He raised concerns.
And he was steadily marginalized, his warnings ignored, his position undermined, his expertise treated as an obstacle rather than an asset.
He became depressed, drinking heavily, ground down by the impossible situation of being a serious scientist inside a company built on a fantasy.
In two thousand thirteen, summoned to give a deposition in a patent lawsuit, facing pressure from a company that seemed to view him as a liability, Ian Gibbons took his own life.
His death is one of the quietest and most heartbreaking moments in the book, a reminder that behind the glossy story of a young billionaire, real people were being destroyed.
When his widow called the company, the response she got was cold.
Theranos seemed more worried about his access to confidential information than about the man who had died.
Now, how do you keep a fraud like this alive for years?
You raise enormous amounts of money, and to do that, you dazzle investors.
Theranos became a master of the staged demonstration.
When potential investors or partners visited, they would be shown the device, a finger would be pricked, blood would be inserted, and the machine would appear to whir to life and produce a result.
But in many cases what visitors saw was theater.
The device might take the sample and appear to process it, while the actual test was run elsewhere on those conventional machines, or the result shown was a stored value rather than a live reading.

In some demonstrations, employees would take the visitor's sample after the guest left the room and run it in the back on commercial analyzers, then present the numbers as though the little Theranos box had produced them.
The company built software to hide error messages so the device would look like it was working smoothly even when it was failing internally.
The whole operation was engineered to control what outsiders saw and to prevent anyone from looking too closely.
The most brazen example of this stagecraft came when Vice President Joe Biden visited the company.
Theranos wanted to show him a gleaming, automated laboratory of the future, a vision of high-tech medicine.
So the company built one that was essentially a stage set.
They created a fake lab, a beautiful room full of their devices arranged impressively, a demonstration space that did not reflect where actual patient testing happened.
The real lab, where real samples were processed on diluted finger pricks and hacked Siemens machines, was hidden away.
Biden toured the showpiece and praised it as the laboratory of the future.
He had no idea he was looking at a facade.
That episode captures the essence of Theranos: an exquisitely constructed image with almost nothing real behind it.
And then there was the board of directors, which may be the single most remarkable feature of the entire saga.
When you look at the people Elizabeth Holmes assembled to sit on the Theranos board, it reads like a roster of American power.
There was George Shultz, the former Secretary of State under Ronald Reagan, a towering figure of the foreign policy establishment.
There was Henry Kissinger, the legendary and controversial former Secretary of State and national security adviser.
There was William Perry, a former Secretary of Defense.
There was Sam Nunn, a former United States senator who had been one of the most influential voices on national security.
Later, General James Mattis, the four-star Marine who would go on to become Secretary of Defense, joined as well.
These were statesmen, diplomats, and generals, men of enormous prestige and gravitas.
And here is the crucial point: almost none of them had any background in medicine, in laboratory science, in diagnostics, or in the biology and chemistry that Theranos claimed to have revolutionized.
They were dazzled by Elizabeth, drawn in by her patriotic language and her vision of American innovation, and they lent her their names and their credibility.
But they were in no position to evaluate whether the technology actually worked.
The board that was supposed to provide oversight was decorative.
It provided the appearance of legitimacy without any of the substance of scrutiny.
Their famous names told the world that Theranos must be real, precisely because such serious people would never attach themselves to something fraudulent.
It was credibility by association, and it worked for years.
With the demos, the board, and the story all in place, the money flooded in and the valuation soared.
Theranos raised hundreds of millions of dollars.

At its peak, the company was valued at roughly nine billion dollars, which on paper made Elizabeth Holmes, who held a large majority of the shares, a paper billionaire, one of the wealthiest self-made women in the world.
She was celebrated everywhere.
She landed on the covers of major magazines.
She gave a widely watched TED talk.
She was profiled in glowing terms, invited to speak alongside presidents, honored as a visionary.
The narrative was irresistible: a young woman, a Stanford dropout, succeeding in the male-dominated world of technology and biotech, on a mission to democratize health care and save lives.
It was exactly the story people wanted to be true, and the more famous she became, the harder it was for anyone to imagine she might be a fraud.
The investors were a who's who of wealth.
Rupert Murdoch, the media mogul, personally invested roughly one hundred twenty-five million dollars, making him one of the largest single backers.
The Walton family, heirs to the Walmart fortune, invested.
The DeVos family, another dynasty of immense wealth, put in tens of millions.
These were sophisticated people with access to any expertise money could buy, and yet they too were swept up.

Part of what happened is that Theranos deliberately avoided the traditional venture capital firms that specialize in biotech, the ones who would have demanded to see peer-reviewed data and independent validation.
Instead, Elizabeth courted wealthy families and individuals who were more easily charmed and less equipped to conduct rigorous technical due diligence.

The pattern repeats throughout the story: the people best positioned to catch the fraud were kept at arm's length, and the people brought close were chosen partly because they would not, or could not, look too hard.
Now we come to the part where the fantasy stopped being confined to boardrooms and demonstrations and started to touch actual human beings.
Theranos struck two enormous partnerships.
One was with Walgreens, the nationwide pharmacy chain, which agreed to put Theranos wellness centers inside its stores, where ordinary people could walk in and get their blood tested.
The other was with Safeway, the grocery chain, which spent hundreds of millions of dollars renovating its stores to add clinics for the same purpose.
These deals were the whole point in a sense, the path to putting Theranos testing in front of millions of consumers.
And they meant that the unreliable technology, the diluted finger pricks and the hacked machines producing erratic results, was now being used on real patients making real medical decisions.
The consequences were exactly what you would fear.
Patients received results that were alarmingly wrong.
People were told their blood indicated serious problems when they were fine, and people were told they were fine when they were not.

Carreyrou documents cases of patients whose test results showed dangerous abnormalities that sent them into a panic, rushing to emergency rooms, undergoing further testing, only to find that the Theranos numbers were simply false.
A woman was told she might be having a miscarriage.
Patients with real conditions got readings so far off that their doctors were misled.
Some results for critical measures, things like potassium levels or calcium or markers that doctors use to make urgent decisions, were wildly inaccurate.
Doctors, trusting the results, could have adjusted medications or ordered unnecessary procedures or missed real dangers.
In blood testing, a wrong number is not a minor glitch.
It can lead directly to harm.
This is the moral center of the whole book: the fraud was no longer just deceiving rich investors, which is bad enough.
It was now gambling with the health of unsuspecting patients who had no idea the technology behind their results did not work.
Inside the company, some employees knew.
They saw the failed proficiency tests, the quality control problems, the results that made no sense, the pressure to release numbers anyway.
And a few of them, at great personal cost, decided they could not stay silent.
This is where the whistleblowers enter, and their courage is the beating heart of the story's resolution.
One of them was Tyler Shultz, and his position was extraordinary, because his grandfather was George Shultz, the former Secretary of State and one of the most prominent members of the Theranos board.
Tyler was young, idealistic, and had joined Theranos partly inspired by his grandfather's belief in the company.
But once inside, working in the lab, he saw things that horrified him.
He saw the company manipulating its proficiency testing, the process by which labs are supposed to prove their accuracy.
He saw quality control failures being ignored.
He raised his concerns internally, going up the chain, and he was rebuffed and treated as a problem.
He even took his concerns to Elizabeth directly by email, and what came back was a scathing response from Sunny Balwani, dripping with condescension and threat.
Tyler faced an agonizing conflict, because his own grandfather was on the board and revered Elizabeth.
He tried to warn his grandfather, and his grandfather, in his loyalty to Elizabeth and his belief in the company, largely did not believe him.
Imagine the pain of that, a young man watching a beloved grandfather side with a con artist over his own family.
Tyler eventually became a confidential source for the journalist investigating the company, and he did so knowing the danger.
Another crucial whistleblower was Erika Cheung, a young scientist who worked alongside Tyler in the lab.
She too witnessed the failures, the manipulation of data, the results that should never have been released to patients.
She saw the company running tests it knew were unreliable and reporting them anyway.
Erika made the brave decision to leave, and later to speak up, and eventually she would contact regulators directly.
These were not senior executives with fortunes to fall back on.
They were young people at the start of their careers, and they put everything on the line because they could not live with what they were seeing.
And the retaliation they faced was ferocious.
Theranos had hired one of the most powerful and feared lawyers in the country, David Boies, the litigator famous for representing the government against Microsoft and for arguing high-profile cases.
Boies and his firm were not only Theranos's lawyers; at one point they took equity in the company, which gave them a personal stake in protecting it.
Boies unleashed an aggressive campaign of legal intimidation against the whistleblowers and against anyone who spoke to the press.
Employees who left were made to sign strict agreements, then hounded with letters threatening ruinous lawsuits if they were suspected of sharing information.
Private investigators followed people.

Tyler Shultz was pursued relentlessly, served with legal threats, watched, and pressured, and the fight cost his family a fortune in legal fees and drove a painful wedge between Tyler and his grandfather.
The company's message to anyone who might talk was clear: we will destroy you.
It takes a special kind of courage to keep going in the face of that, knowing the full weight of a nine-billion-dollar company and its feared attorneys is aimed at crushing you.
Meanwhile, the man who would ultimately bring the whole thing down was building his case one terrified source at a time.
John Carreyrou, the author of the book and its central journalistic hero, was an investigative reporter at the Wall Street Journal.
He got a tip from a source who was skeptical of the Theranos story, and he began pulling the thread.

What he found, as he dug, was a company obsessed with secrecy, a technology that did not seem to work, and a trail of frightened former employees who wanted to talk but were petrified of the legal consequences.
Carreyrou's work was slow, careful, and painstaking.
He had to earn the trust of people who had signed draconian agreements, who were being watched, who risked losing everything by speaking to him.
He cultivated these sources gently, protected their identities, corroborated their accounts, and gradually assembled a picture of systematic fraud and patient endangerment.
He got doctors and patients to describe the wrong results.
He got lab workers to describe the diluted samples and the failed quality controls.
He built the story brick by brick against a company that was doing everything in its power to stop him.
And Theranos fought back hard against Carreyrou too.
The company's lawyers pressured the Journal, threatened litigation, and disputed his reporting at every turn.
Then Elizabeth made a move that revealed just how connected and confident she had become.
Remember that Rupert Murdoch was one of her largest investors, having put in around one hundred twenty-five million dollars.
Murdoch also happened to own News Corp, the parent company of the Wall Street Journal, the very paper Carreyrou worked for.
So Elizabeth went over the journalist's head and appealed directly to Murdoch, meeting with him and trying to get him to intervene and kill the story to protect his investment.
This is a stunning moment.
She was asking the owner of the newspaper to quash an investigation into her own company.
And here is the redemptive twist: Murdoch declined.

Despite having a hundred twenty-five million dollars at stake, despite his direct financial interest in Theranos surviving, he told Elizabeth that he trusted his editors and would let the journalism run its course.
He did not spike the story.
Whatever one thinks of Rupert Murdoch, in this instance the wall between the business side and the newsroom held, and the truth was allowed to come out.
In October of two thousand fifteen, the Wall Street Journal published Carreyrou's front-page exposé.

The article laid out the core of it: that Theranos was running only a small fraction of its tests on its own proprietary device, that it was relying heavily on conventional commercial machines, that its technology produced questionable results, and that former employees had grave concerns about accuracy and patient safety.
It landed like a bomb.
For the first time, the carefully constructed image cracked in public.
Theranos responded with fury and denial, insisting the reporting was wrong, attacking Carreyrou's credibility, and doubling down on its claims.
Elizabeth went on television to defend the company and dismiss the allegations as the work of a disgruntled few.
For a moment it was unclear who the public would believe: the celebrated visionary or the newspaper.
But once the truth started coming out, it could not be stopped, because now the regulators moved in.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, the federal body that oversees clinical laboratories, inspected the Theranos lab in Newark, California.
What they found was damning.
Their inspection report described conditions so serious that they posed, in the regulators' own language, immediate jeopardy to patient health and safety.
The lab was a mess of improper procedures, unqualified personnel, faulty quality controls, and devices that did not meet accuracy standards.
This was not a journalist's opinion anymore; it was the federal government's official finding.
The consequences cascaded quickly.
Regulators moved to revoke the lab's certification and to bar Elizabeth Holmes from operating a laboratory.
Theranos was forced to void or correct enormous numbers of test results.

In fact, the company ended up voiding or correcting around two years' worth of results from its Edison devices, essentially admitting that a vast swath of the tests it had given to real patients could not be trusted.
Think about what that means.
Thousands upon thousands of medical results, results people had used to make decisions about their bodies and their treatments, were simply erased as unreliable.
The partnerships collapsed.
Walgreens, which had put Theranos wellness centers in its stores and had invested heavily, cut ties and shut down the testing centers, then later sued to recover its money.
Safeway had already quietly backed away after its own doubts, having wasted hundreds of millions on clinics it never fully opened.
The magazine covers that had crowned Elizabeth turned into cautionary tales.
The nine-billion-dollar valuation evaporated.

Forbes, which had once celebrated her as the youngest self-made female billionaire, revised her net worth to essentially nothing, because her fortune was entirely tied up in the worthless stock of a company built on fraud.
Investors who had poured in hundreds of millions faced the reality that their money was gone.
Then came the legal reckoning, and it unfolded over years.

The Securities and Exchange Commission charged Elizabeth Holmes and Sunny Balwani with massive fraud, accusing them of deceiving investors with false claims about the technology, the company's finances, and its business relationships.
The SEC detailed how they had lied about the device's capabilities, exaggerated revenue, and misrepresented the deals with Walgreens and the military.

Elizabeth settled with the SEC, paying a penalty, giving up control of the company, and accepting a ban from serving as an officer or director of a public company for years, though notably that civil settlement did not require her to admit wrongdoing.
But the more serious hammer was still coming.
Federal prosecutors brought criminal charges.
Elizabeth Holmes and Sunny Balwani were indicted on multiple counts of wire fraud and conspiracy to commit wire fraud, accused of defrauding both investors and patients.
The criminal trials came after the book was written, but the story reaches its conclusion there, and it is worth telling you how it ended, because it completes the arc.
After a long and closely watched trial, Elizabeth Holmes was convicted on multiple counts of fraud against investors.
The jury found that she had knowingly deceived the people who funded her company.
Sunny Balwani was tried separately and convicted on even more counts.
Both were sentenced to years in federal prison.
Elizabeth, who during her trial had married and had children and tried to recast herself, ultimately reported to prison to serve a sentence of more than eleven years.
Sunny received a sentence in a similar range.
The woman who had once been compared to Steve Jobs, who had graced magazine covers and charmed statesmen and raised nearly a billion dollars, ended up behind bars.
Theranos itself dissolved, its assets liquidated, its patents and its promises worth nothing.
The great dream was over, and the reckoning was as complete as the rise had been dazzling.
So what does Bad Blood actually teach us, when the last page is turned and the story settles?
Carreyrou is careful, and so should we be, to draw the right lessons, because it would be easy to take the wrong ones.
The first and most important lesson is about the line between bold vision and outright fraud, and it is this: that line is not blurry, at least not when real patients' lives are at stake.
In Silicon Valley there is a celebrated culture of ambition, of promising the impossible, of fake it till you make it, of moving fast and figuring out the details later.
And for some products, a photo-sharing app, a social network, a piece of software, that culture can be relatively harmless.
You can ship a buggy version, iterate, and improve.
But medicine is not software.
When your product tells a patient whether they have cancer, whether they are having a miscarriage, whether their heart is in danger, there is no room to fake it till you make it.
A wrong result is not a bug to be patched in the next release.
It is a person making a life-or-death decision based on a lie.
Elizabeth Holmes took the ethos of the tech industry and applied it to human health, and in that context, faking it is not visionary boldness.
It is a crime.
Carreyrou never lets us forget that patients got dangerously false results, and that is the difference between a founder who overpromises and one who commits fraud.
The second lesson is that charisma is not competence, and we confuse the two at our peril.
Elizabeth Holmes was extraordinarily charismatic.
She had the voice, the stare, the story, the uniform, the ability to make people feel they were part of something historic.
And that charisma did an enormous amount of work that evidence should have done.
Investors gave money because she was compelling, not because they had verified the technology.
Board members lent their names because she was inspiring, not because they understood the science.
Journalists wrote glowing profiles because the narrative was irresistible, not because they had confirmed the claims.
Over and over, the force of her personality substituted for proof.
The world wanted a hero, and she looked and sounded exactly like one.
But sounding like a genius and being one are entirely different things, and an enormous fraud was able to grow precisely in the gap between the two.
The third lesson, closely related, is that a great story is not the same as the truth.
The Theranos story was perfect.
A young woman, a Stanford dropout, afraid of needles, driven to democratize health care and let no one say goodbye to a loved one too soon.
It had emotion, it had a hero, it had a villain in the old, needle-wielding medical establishment, and it had a beautiful promise.
It was so good that people fell in love with it and stopped checking whether it was real.
But a narrative, no matter how moving, is not data.
The elegance of a pitch tells you nothing about whether the machine works.
Carreyrou's book is, in a sense, a defense of the unglamorous virtue of verification, of the boring, essential work of asking to see the evidence and then actually looking at it.
The fourth lesson is the one that should keep us up at night, and it is about the failure of the guardrails.
Think of all the checks that were supposed to catch something like this.
There was a board of directors, whose job is oversight.
There were investors, who are supposed to conduct due diligence.
There were corporate partners, Walgreens and Safeway, giant companies with resources and their own experts.
There was the press, whose job is scrutiny.
And there were regulators.
Almost every one of these guardrails failed, at least for a long time.
The board was composed of statesmen with no relevant expertise who provided credibility instead of scrutiny.
Many investors were chosen precisely because they would not look too hard, and they skipped the technical due diligence that traditional biotech investors would have demanded.
The corporate partners were so eager to be part of the exciting future that they let their skepticism be overridden.
Some of the press amplified the myth rather than questioning it.
In case after case, hype did the work that evidence and diligence were supposed to do.
The system of checks that we assume protects us turned out to be astonishingly easy to bypass when everyone wanted to believe.
And yet, and this matters, the guardrails did eventually work, thanks to individuals who refused to go along.
The whole thing came down not because the system automatically corrected itself, but because specific brave people made specific hard choices.
Tyler Shultz risked his family relationships and endured legal terror to tell the truth.
Erika Cheung walked away from a job and then spoke to regulators.
Other former employees, frightened but principled, talked to a reporter.
John Carreyrou did months of careful, courageous journalism against a company trying to destroy him.
And Rupert Murdoch, despite a hundred twenty-five million reasons to bury the story, let his journalists do their work.
The lesson there is quietly hopeful: institutions are only as good as the individuals willing to act with integrity inside them.
The exposure of Theranos was a triumph not of some abstract system but of human courage, of young people and reporters who decided that the truth mattered more than their own safety or comfort.
There is a final, subtler takeaway worth sitting with.
Elizabeth Holmes may well have started out believing in her vision.

Many who study the case wonder how much she was a cynical con artist from day one versus a true believer who could not admit failure and then lied, and lied again, and kept lying to cover the gap between her dream and reality until the lies became a monstrous fraud.
That is perhaps the most human and most cautionary part of the whole case.

It suggests that the road to enormous fraud is often paved with a series of smaller deceptions, each one rationalized as temporary, each one told to protect a dream that felt too important to abandon.
Just a little longer, just this one demo faked, just this one result fudged, and then the technology will catch up and it will all have been worth it.
Except the technology never caught up, and the lies never stopped, and real people were hurt.
The willingness to deceive, even in service of a goal you believe is noble, is exactly how good intentions curdle into crime.
Bad Blood, in the end, is a thriller, and Carreyrou tells it with the pace and tension of one.

But it is also a warning about our own appetites, our hunger for visionary heroes, our eagerness to believe a beautiful story, our tendency to mistake confidence for competence and charisma for truth.

Elizabeth Holmes built a nine-billion-dollar company on a machine that never worked, and she did it not entirely by force but with the enthusiastic help of everyone who wanted the dream to be real and could not bear to check.

The next time someone in a black turtleneck, or whatever the next costume of genius turns out to be, stands on a stage and tells you they are going to change the world, the lesson of this book is simple and hard.
Believe the evidence, not the story.
Ask to see it work.
And remember that when human lives hang in the balance, faking it until you make it is not the mark of a visionary.
It is the beginning of a fraud.
That is the story of Theranos, and that is why, years later, we are still learning from it.
